name: Denmark-Digital Identity Infrastructure Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Reduced dependence on Apple/Google → Systemic: Improved digital sovereignty, resilience, and continuity of service → Strategic: Enhanced strategic autonomy and reduced supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop a platform-neutral, open-standards-based authentication model that leverages FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect, reducing dependence on proprietary Apple/Google platforms.
Implement a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path to provide an alternative to mobile OS duopoly, enhancing continuity of service and digital sovereignty.

Establish a public-private partnership to develop and certify a platform-neutral, open-standards-based identity infrastructure, promoting supplier diversity and reducing dependence on foreign technology suppliers.
review: Controls Digital Sovereignty vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the feasibility of implementing a new authentication model in existing systems without disrupting user experience.

name: Denmark-Procurement Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Altered procurement processes → Systemic: Increased supplier diversity, reduced dependence on foreign technology suppliers → Strategic: Enhanced digital sovereignty and resilience.
options: Influence AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement documents to include platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language.

Engage with EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework to align Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in.
Develop procurement language and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude from AltID and related digital identity planning.
review: Controls Procurement Neutrality vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential resistance from existing operators due to incumbent interests.

name: Denmark-Regulatory Framework Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Revised regulatory framework → Systemic: Increased digital sovereignty, resilience, and continuity of service → Strategic: Enhanced strategic autonomy and reduced supplier concentration risk.
options: Reframe the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk to address privacy and democratic accountability concerns.
Develop a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen that promotes platform-neutrality, fallback access, and continuity, reducing dependence on foreign technology suppliers.

Engage with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework to promote alignment with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in.
review: Controls Regulatory Framework vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential regulatory friction due to existing laws and regulations.

name: Denmark-Technical Demonstrator Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Non-production technical demonstrators → Systemic: Credible technical evidence for platform-neutral and fallback-capable access models → Strategic: Enhanced digital sovereignty, resilience, and continuity of service.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator that authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform.
Explore a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path to reduce dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether, enhancing continuity of service and digital sovereignty.
Create a non-production technical demonstrator that showcases a certified fallback access path for critical public authentication, reducing dependence on foreign technology suppliers.
review: Controls Technical Demonstrators vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential financing uncertainty due to project funding constraints.

name: Denmark-Project Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Project governance structure → Systemic: Enhanced digital sovereignty, resilience, and continuity of service → Strategic: Reduced supplier concentration risk.
options: Establish a project team with diverse expertise in mobile security, authentication protocols, regulatory compliance, policy, public affairs, and technical leads for demonstrator work.

Develop a formal governance structure that includes a lead researcher, regulatory specialist, policy coordinator, technical lead, and additional engineers or contractors to cover web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation.
review: Controls Project Governance vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential institutional inertia due to existing project governance structures.

name: Digital Sovereignty Catalyst
consequences: Immediate: Enhanced public awareness and advocacy → Systemic: Increased digital sovereignty, resilience, and continuity of service → Strategic: Reduced supplier concentration risk.
options: Launch a public awareness campaign to educate citizens about the benefits of digital sovereignty and platform-neutral access models.
Develop strategic partnerships with civil society organizations, privacy advocates, and academic researchers to amplify the message and build momentum.
Create a coalition of stakeholders to advocate for policy changes that promote digital sovereignty and reduce dependence on foreign technology suppliers.
review: Controls Public Awareness vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential resistance from existing operators due to incumbent interests.

name: Interoperability Enabler
consequences: Immediate: Improved interoperability among digital identity systems → Systemic: Increased digital sovereignty, resilience, and continuity of service → Strategic: Reduced supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop a set of open standards for digital identity that enable seamless interoperability among different systems and platforms.

Establish a technical working group to promote the adoption of these standards and ensure their alignment with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in.
Create a certification program for digital identity providers that meet the new interoperability standards.
review: Controls Interoperability vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential regulatory friction due to existing laws and regulations.

name: Fallback Authentication Framework
consequences: Immediate: Established fallback authentication path → Systemic: Increased digital sovereignty, resilience, and continuity of service → Strategic: Reduced supplier concentration risk.
options: Develop a robust fallback authentication framework that provides an alternative to mobile OS duopoly, enhancing continuity of service and digital sovereignty.
Implement a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path to reduce dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether.
Create a set of guidelines for implementing fallback authentication in existing systems, ensuring seamless integration with new platforms.
review: Controls Fallback Authentication vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential financing uncertainty due to project funding constraints.

name: Digital Identity Architecture Revamp

options: Develop a new digital identity architecture that promotes platform-neutral access models, fallback authentication paths, and interoperability among different systems and platforms.
Establish a technical working group to ensure the alignment of this new architecture with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in.
Create a set of guidelines for implementing the revised architecture in existing systems, ensuring seamless integration with new platforms.
review: Controls Digital Identity Architecture vs. Supplier Concentration Risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential institutional inertia due to existing project governance structures.
